sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 38-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of right shoulder pain, initially attributed to a recent work project in the backyard.
He reports soreness in the right shoulder, particularly on the outside, which persists despite Advil and icing.
He has a history of similar issues with the same shoulder, dating back to playing baseball in his youth.
The pain is exacerbated by movement and is most pronounced on the outside of the shoulder.
He denies any numbness in the arm or hands.
He has been experiencing some pain at night, particularly when sleeping on the affected side.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of playing baseball, which resulted in some previous issues with the right shoulder.
Review of systems: The patient denies any numbness in the arm or hands.
Current medications: Advil as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 97.8°F, heart rate is 62 beats per minute, respirations are 16 breaths per minute, blood pressure is 116/84 mmHg, and oxygen saturation is 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
There is no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
The patient has a good hand grip and intact sensation to touch in the right arm.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's right shoulder pain is most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; status: confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Tylenol for pain management.
I ordered an MRI to further evaluate the shoulder.
I referred the patient to physical therapy for approximately 6-8 weeks.
If symptoms do not improve, we will consider a steroid injection for the shoulder.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in 2 weeks to assess the effectiveness of the treatment plan.
